THE

[RISH BOOK LOVER

Vor. VI. JULY, 1915. No. 12

JOHN DAVIS WHITE AND CASHEL PRINTING.

John Davis White, F.S.A., solicitor, newspaper proprietor and
sub-Librarian of the Cashel Diocesan Library, to whom we owe most
of our knowledge of the history of printing in this famous old city,
.was the son of B. N. White, J.P., for many years deputy mayor
under the old unreformed Corporation. He was born at Conahy, Kil-
kenny, on 22nd May, 1820, and when quite a boy removed with his
family to Cashel, where he spent all his life. He inherited his anti-
quarian and literary tastes from his father, as did his brother, Rev.
H. V. White, who died in 1865, and his sister, Hannah, who became
Mrs. Lawless, and published a volume of ‘‘ Verses: Sacred and Mis-
cellaneous,” London, 1853. In an interesting article which he con-
tributed to ‘‘The Cashel Gazette,”” 26th August, 1865, reprinted 1n
John Power’s ‘‘Irish Literary Inquirer,”’ he tells us how he pur-
chased, in 1856, an old wooden, press on which the ‘‘ Clonmel Herald "’
had been printed, and for his own amusement issued a few broad-
sides entitled ‘‘The Amateur Press.”” In the last four months of
1861 he issued four monthly numbers of the ‘‘Cashel Advertiser,’’
and out of -that grew his once well known ‘‘ Cashel Gazette.”” The
first number of this was issued on Saturday, l4th May, 1864, price
2d., unstamped. It consisted of a single sheet, headed with a block
of ‘“The Rock,”’ containing five wide columns and printed on one
side of the paper only. The imprint is ‘‘ Printed for the Proprietor,
John Davis White, by Patrick Bourke, at 44, Main Street, Cashel.”
No. 2 was again a single sheet, but printed on both sides, the re-
verse being entitled ‘‘Supplement.”” With No. 3 it expands to four

" pages, the last three being in the nature of supplied ‘‘stereo.”” With

| No. 5 the first page is divided into six columns, his *‘ Cashel .of the
4 Kings” commences to be comtinued weekly, and the printer’s name

% changes to Burke, to revert in No. 18 to the original form of spelling.
i B " One judges that the printer was, to say the least, cccentric, and it
00mes as no surprise to find that there were occasional rumpuses in
 ‘ the office, one of which led to thoe unfortunate printer being fined 10s.

=

194 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

and costs on 4th October. Mr. Davis contributed to 1t ‘ Walkg
About Cashel,”” ““The Bohereen Glas,”” and the ‘‘Legend of the
Golden Bridge,”” with other local antiquarian sketches. With the
second year the price is reduced to 1}d., the paper is curtailed both
in the length and breadth, and reduced to 5 cols., but all four pages
are printed on a Columban machine which he had installed. Hence-
forth until his decease its career was that of a provincial sheet, but
brightened with many an essay and sketch, for its proprietor wielded a

~ facile pen, both in prose and verse. In addition to the works men-

tioned below, he published for private circulation ‘‘ A History of the

- White Family’’ and ‘‘Sixty Years in Cashel.” Writing wo his friend

Mr. James Coleman, F.R.S.A.I., of Queenstown, under date 26th
October, 1889, Mr. White says: ‘‘ You have no idea of the trouble
I have had to get these books printed after hours, when a weekly
newspaper has been printed off. For the last bwenty-ﬁve'years Cr so
I have educated boys wno were very ignorant, who, as soon as thsy
knew a little, were ready to leave me, the business not allowing me
to retain the services of those whom I thus brought up. Some of my
boys are doing very well in America and Dublin, and one whom I took
without a shoe to his foot is now a counsellor!’”” Mr. White died on
14th June, 1893, and was buried in the Cathedral yard not far from
the Library he loved and used so well, and his paper did not long
survive him.

BLOW’S BOOKS AND BIBLES.

There is in the library of the Assembly’s College here an early
Belfast printed volume which has never been recorded. It is entitled
“The Sin of Withholding Tribute by running of Goods, Concealing
Excise, etc. Addressed to the Treading part of the Nation. By
g J. B., Master of Arts, Belfast. Printed by James Blow, 1707.”
§ dto., pp. 46. On verso of title page is ‘Imprimatur Edw. Down and

pages (already recorded.) It contains the following inscription:

not a strong point with the old printer. But what renders the little
volume more interesting still is that the first ‘‘end paper” is a
sheet of two reversed leaves of the Old Testament, showing 2nd Book
of Samuel, running thus: 1st leaf, Chaps. xiv. and =xv.; 2nd Ileaf,
Chaps. vi., vii. and viii. I have carefully compared them with the
Psalm Book printed by Blow in 1711, and I am. oconvinced that the
“type used is the same in both cases. [ was particularly struck with
@ misprint therein, viz,, ‘‘moe’ for ‘‘more,”” which turned my
thoughts in the direction of the much-debated ‘‘Sin on More’’ Bible,
and led me to look up some authorities on the matter.  The first
person to mention the printing of the Bible in Belfast was Henry
Joy, who, in the ‘‘Historical Collections’’ published by Berwick in
1817, says, at p. 80, ‘‘ After the death of Neill the business was con-
tinued by Blow, who, about 1704, printed the first edition of the
Bible in Ireland.”” Now Joy, who was a -boy of five when Blow died,
was in the printing and paper-making trades, like Blow’s sons and
successors, and was admittedly the highest authority on the history
of Belfast in his own day or since. He is, therefore, a credible witness.
Then Jobn Hodgson, who first printed the anecdote regarding the
“8in on More” Bible in the ‘‘ Ulster Journal of Archesology,” Vol.
3, 1855, whence Bohm, in his edition of ‘‘Lowndes’ Bibliographer’s
Manual,”’ copied it—and incorrectly at that—had the anecdote from
Blow’s grandson. This, combined with the evidence of the misprint
before us, the similarity of type, and the contemporary character of
the leaves, should at least lead us to suspend judgment and not
make such sweeping statements as a contributor did in your second

AR - volume, page 109. Without being dogmatical and saying these leaves
2 ~ represent all we have of Blow’s * Sin on More” Bible, one may

| :_[ - safely assert that these recent dl.soovemes bring us a step nearer con- *
fﬁrma;taon of the truth of these traditions, and the solution of 'vhe
e fmystea-y that hangs around Blow’s Belfast Bible. 7%

BALLADE OF THE COUNTRY OF THE YOUNG. i

There is a kingdom cool and green, ' e

Washed by the ever-moaning sea,
- From whose wild surf, with furious mien, =%

Lir’s war-hounds struggle to be free. , | | £

The tempest breaks on tower and tree, e

Exultantly proud wings are flung; ey

Joy in the storm the watchers see— ; | _‘ T *
: It is the country of the young.

Swart Cromwell’s wrath, proud Strafford’s fears,
The Boyne s despair and Limerick’s tears—
They fade, they die, as runes long sung;

| THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.
THE LITERARY SOCIETILES.

The National ILiterary Society of Dublin has just issued its annual
report for the Session 1914-15, and a most satisfactory and encourag-
ing dooument it is. Notwithstanding the unexampled public stress
and strain, its members have brought to a successful termination
which,
gquantity and quality, will bear comparison with that of any previous
session. One of the lectures, as noted elsewhere, has had the unusual
honour of ‘being published in ‘‘ The Nineteenth Century.”” In addi-
tion it closes with an increased list of members and a healthy balance
to its credit. Much of the Society’s success 1s undoubtedly due to the
labours of the energetic secretary, Mr. W. A. Henderson, who, in addi-
tion to his other duties during the session, has found time to con-
tribute a series of scholarly and informative articles to ‘‘ The Athen-
sgoum ’’> on the Arthurian cycle of legends and their connection with
Ireland.

Now that the ordinary business of the session has concluded, the

Irish Literary Society has directed its efforts into other channels with

much success. Last month, under its auspices, members and friends
gave a dramatic entertainment at the Cripplegate Theatre on behalf
of the funds of the Munster Fusiliers Old Comrades’ Association. T_he
Wilde’s comedy, ‘A
and Lady Gregory’s evergreen ‘¢ Work-
Both pieces went with an eclat that left nothing to

pieces chosen for representation were Oscar

house Ward.”’

~ be desired, and a most deserving association was enriched to the ex-

tent of twenty-five pounds.
This month was held the annual exhibition of Irish Arts and

Crafts, which, growing in importance every succeeding year, attracted
‘a larger number of visitors than .ever before.
was performed by the President, Miss Eleanor Hull, who, in her ad-
‘dress, dwelt with pleasure on the increasing spread of artistic and

Amongst the exhibits were sketches in colours and black and white

thanked the members for the enthusiasm with which they had pursued

: EDITOR’S GOSSIP. =

From the First Annual Report of the Plunkett House Co-operative : .-;_’:_‘-?.- !

Reference Library, recently issued, we learn that, like so many simi-

lar institutions, it has been heavily hit by the war. The librarian *3:. -

expresses his obligations to Sir Horace Plunkett and Mr. George R

Russell for large numbers of books lent from their private collections,

and hopes to be able next year to issue from time to time select
bibliographies of various branches of their subject. Already he nas a
card catalogue of some six thousand warks filled up in detail. A very

promising beginning.

To the ‘ Nineteenth Century’’ for June Sir Honace Plunkett con-
tributes a remarkable article, originally delivered as a lecture before

5 In bringing another volume to a close I ocannot refrain from ex- L
~ pressing to my increasing circle of friends and supporters my thanks R

for all the assistance accorded me during the past year. There is no
need to dwell here upon the course of public events during that event-

o ——— e

bined to do for the rural districts of Ireland what the Georgian
i Society has done for the old mansions of the metropolis, and have t
gucceeded admirably. Here they had, so to say, virgin soil to oxplore,
~ and their task has been on the whole a pleasanter one, for whilst the
- Dublin houses have in so many cases fallen from their high estate, P
~ the country mansions here depicted have, on the whole, maintained
§ ' their primal lustre untarnished, and preserved their domestic ameni-
ties intact. The volume is no mere architectural handbook, but an
{  interesting record of fair women and brave men who have at various
= * {imes inHlabited these grand old mansions and stately homes. Tt" 25s .
2 ~ contains a wealth of genealogical and biographical information, the
Er";j ) collection of which must have entailed considerable research and much |
A:f‘ of it not easily to be found elsewhere. In addition, one or two little
~ details dear to the heart of the book lover mnestle in its pages, such
~as the fact that the fine library at Caledon ‘‘contains a collection ef
nem':@y 7,000 volumes which belonged to Bishop Percy and bear his
5 _a»utograpfh,”. which were s6ld by his executors to the second K ]l of
{ i la.]edon and that Archbishop Bolton, in 1744, beque&thed lus ﬁne
{L”?ltbﬁm to tho clergy of his diocese, ‘‘to be preserved for ever a“v “"
3 -ashel for their use. 7  Qwing to the courtesy of their respec

~__" ..j:-.E

v r,'*-"_j._".s‘, ‘many famouﬁ portraits and O'bh@ﬁ paintings have been hé

- ful time. Suffice it to say that whilst in many cases older and better _

;:f,’ known organs have been submerged, I.B.L. has, so far, weathered 0 :

g the storm, thanks entirely to the loyalty of contributors and sub-

. scribers. I have been assured by many readers that they have found

a welcome counter-attraction in its pages to the sometimes woeful

. npews in the papers, and I may say it has been Tead with pleasure in S
the trenches. 1 hope with the new volume to introduce some new : |
features that I trust will prove acceptable to my readers. =

204 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

published for the first time, which renders additional interest wo a
book, which, as the changing condition of things proceeds, is sure
to increase in value as the years roll on. That it i1s beautifully
printed goes without saying, and the illustrations are reproduced in
the highest style of art, some of the interiors being remarkably well
done. Wo rejoice to see such a fine array of subseribers names
headed by Her Majesty the Queen, and congratulate the editors on
the results of their learned, artistic, and, in the best sense of the
word, patriotic undertaking.

MinniE’'s Bismor AND OTEER StoRrIES. DBy Geo. A. Birmingham.
(Hodder and Stoughton.) 6s. An excellent collection of sketches
exhibiting the author in many moods, but still with a strong propen-
sity for poking fun at Government inspectors and piers. The title
story is the thinnest of the lot , and the curious study, ‘‘ Bed Clothes,”
the most original and aan'es@illé. In ¢ Ileanor’s Enterprise’ we find
the germ of the author’s first attempt at playwriting. For the others
we have one or two strongly-drawn characters, most probably the fruit
of his recent visit to the land of the doll-a&s, and some humorous 1n-
cidents which show that no writer has so well realized the topsy
tu-rvejtlom of Irish politics of the day before yesterday with its dis-
patch riding and gun running.

Porms. By Ethne of “The Nation.” (M. H. Gill and Son.) 3s. 6d.
Some of these poems by a lady who still desires to retain her anony-
mity appeared many years ago in the old ‘“Nation’ and ‘‘ The Celt.”
Fired by the example of ‘‘ those gallant gentlemen of ’48 who breathed
a new soul into Ireland,”’” she wrote on the spur of the moment as

the fancy seized her of the stirring heroic deeds of the past and

homelier incidents that came within her own purview. Simple ballad
stanzas and touching lyrics, they well deserved collection, and thanks
to the admiring assiduity of Mr. J. deL. Smyth, who performed thabt
task, they are now assured of a mew lease of popularity. The volume
iz excellently printed by the Dundalgan Press.

Tae Reoiter’s TreEAsURY of Irish Prose and Verse. Compiled and
Edited by Alfred Perceval Gnaves and Guy Pertwee. (Geo. Routledge

~and Sons.) 3s. 6d. When two gentlemen of such eminence, the one

as poet and critic, the other as a cultured elocutionist, collaborate in

| a work such as this, success is assured. This is as fine a collection of
- Imish verse and prose suited for platform declamation as it has ever

T THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 205

been our lot to peruse. The editors have been fortunate in being
allowed their choice of selections from so many of our younger writers,
some of whom, for the first time, attain pride of place in an anthology.
Happy are the present-day reciters, and indeed readers and students
in having such an excellent compilation placed at their disposal. The
elaborate introduction by Mr. Graves is a graphic, well-balanced
critical survey of the whole field of Anglo-Irish literature for close on
a couple of centuries. The work .is well printed and handsomely
turned out, and containing, as it does, over 500 pages, is remarkably
_cheap at the price. We only notice one or two slips, correctly stated
in the notes, in which, by the way, Mr. Pertwee confuses the two
Edward Walshs, the physician and the schoolmaster, and omits the
name of John Keegan from the index.

AssocIATION Boogks. I have a volume entitled ‘¢ Occasional Papers

fly leaf bears this inscription: ¢ Thomas Andrews, M.D., etc., etc.,
etc. From his old and affectionate Friend O’Hagan. 11 Mar., ’84.”
It is an interesting memento of the friendship of two of the most
distinguished men, the one in law, the other in science, Belfast has
ever produced. They were both born in that city within a year of
{| each other; both died 1n the same year—that following the presenta-
§  tion—and both find suitable mention in the pages of the Dictionary
§ of National Biography. Whilst the one was known .to his associates
by the appellation of ‘‘Silken Thomas,” the other was called (by his
- students) by the familiar name of * Tommy.”” It is strange that no
biography of O’Hagan has appeared. I think I saw it stated long
 ago that his son-in-law, Judge John O’Hagan, was about to under-
take it, but his death a few years after prevented its completion.

know that Theobald A. Purcell, who edited * The Irish Monthly |

nell and the old Irelanders.

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 207

gularly defective memory. It is related of her that she often forgot
ber own name; and that when wupon a certain occasion someone
thought it necessary to remind her that Mr. Vesey was her second
husband, the oblivious lady exclaimed in surprise, ‘Bless me, my
dear! d had forgotten all about itl’”

St. Stephen’s Rectory, C.-onM. J. B. McGOVERN.

A writer in the ‘ Times Literary Supplement’’ for 10th June
says of this lady: ‘‘Mrs. Elizabeth Vesey, ‘the Sylph,’ was a bles-
sed antithesis. This slipshod, informal old lady, who made to shine
but shone not, genuinely loved people and company, and was loved
in return. She had imagination, could thrill to the daring of Voltaire

~and enjoy a thunderstorm. ‘Few people,” she said, ‘give themselves

time to be friends,’ and she was one of the few. And she it was who
in genial raillery of the slovenly old humorist Benjamin Stillingfleet
originated the original of bas bleu: ‘Pho, pho,” she entreated him,
‘don’t mind dress! Come in your blue stockings!’?”’

CatHOLIO BOOKS PRINTED IN BELFAST. Amongst the Hope Collec-

tion now in my possession there are two works with the imprint of

Joseph Smyth that are not usually known. They are both clean and
well printed, plainly and stoutly bound in leather. The first 1s:
‘““The New Testament of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, trans-
lated out of the Latin Vulgate, diligently compared with the Original
Greek, and first published by the English College of Rhemes. Anno
1582. Newly revised and corrected by the Clementine edition of the
Scriptures. = With annotations for clearing up modern controversies

| in religion and other difficulties of Holy Writ. Belfast: Printed by

Joseph Smyth, 54, High Street. 1825. Belfast: Stereotyped by M.
Downing, 1825.”” Belfast has thus the credit of printing for circula-
tion a Catholic version of the New Testament four years before

T a ~ Catholic Emancipation. The other is a muca rarer book: ‘‘The Poor
| Man’s Manual of Devotions, or The Devout Christian’s Daily Com-
‘panion, etc. Belfast: Joseph Smyth, Printer, High Street. 1835.”
§ The remarkable feature of this book is the set of wood engravings

_'inumjbemng 36, and specially labelled * Smyth’s Belfast Edition.”
‘ ~ They are really fine pieces of work, ecffectively drawn and oarried
4§ out with perfeot instructive detail. The altar and vestments .are of .
28 —the time, every feature of the Mass is shown with an inset of the

that of Mrs. Helen Concannon, M.A., of Salthill, Galway, as the P
best. It will be published in October. | A

Mr. Arnold Graves, author of ‘‘ Clytemnestra,’” * Prince Patrick,”’
and other works in prose and verse, and a member of the gifted
literary brotherhood, is bringing out a volume of ‘“ War Poems’’ with
Mr. Murray. Those who have read them say *“ such stirring verse
should stimulate recruitd ng.”

That graceful poetess Helen Lanyon (Mrs. Carrington Smith) has
2 new book of vemse entitled ‘¢ Fairy Led’’ in the press. It will be
illustrated by Miss B. S. Duffin, and published by W. and G. Baird,
Royal Avenue, Belfast. Price, 1s. 2d. post free.

OBITUARY.

Tororey O’Nein Lane died on 8th June at Tournafulla, Co.
Limerick, and was buried at Brosna, Co. Kerry. He was born at
Templeglantine, Co. Limerick, 63 years ago, and became a mnational
school teacher there. In 1877 he was appointed a clerk in the In-
corporated Law Society, London, and according to some accounts,
afterwards acted as Paris correspondent to several English news-
papers. He published ‘“Round Erin: or Highways and. Byeways in
Ireland,” N.D.; ‘‘An English-Irish Dictionary,”’ 1904, a larger
odition of which is announced in our advertising pages. In the last
letter we received from him he wrote in reference to this new work :
T have already spent on the book £2,600 on the collection of
materisuls during the greater partv of my life; £300 on compilation,
and £350 on incidental expemses and the cost of living for the past
seven years, which I have devoted exclusively to the Dictionary.
e’y Tn 1913 the Government, after a full inquiry into the value of
my work, made me a grant of £250. I shall never. get back half
the money I have spent on it, but this will not worry me if I can
bring it out in good time.” Though not a great scholar, he was an
earnost and enthusiastic worker, and the new edition of his Diction-
ary will he hig best monument. = |

¢ Dublin. Weekly Freeman,”’ entitled ¢ The Little Corporal.”’
